ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general whitebait food product class; no specific deep-fried crispy whitebait entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated crispy whitebait entry. Code A0CJW for small pelagic fish products used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for crispy whitebait as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of crispy whitebait in Singapore is approximately 150 g, served as a shared starter or side dish at a Zi Char or seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Whitebait, fried — Composition of Foods Integrated Dataset (CoFID) / UK Nutrient Databank; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Fish, whitebait, fried (NDB No. 15225)
nutrient_source.organization: UK Food Standards Agency / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for fried whitebait (NDB 15225 / FDC ID 175168) used as primary nutrient reference. Values represent whole small fish deep-fried, which closely matches the Singaporean Zi Char preparation. Minor adaptation: sodium adjusted upward by approximately 10% to reflect typical restaurant seasoning. IMR Malaysia MyFCD does not carry a dedicated crispy whitebait entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source with a direct fried-whitebait match.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fish and seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on deep-fried fish products; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crispy whitebait. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous deep-fried fish products with light batter coating, which are predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate. Deep-fried fish products in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature consistently show low GI (range 35–50) due to high protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying. GL per serving = (45 × 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.5 serving factor) / 100 ≈ 3.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seasoning and deep-frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 75 g per sitting, as a 150 g serving provides approximately 780 mg sodium, representing about 34% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Crispy whitebait has a low glycemic index (estimated 45) and very low carbohydrate content (~4.5 g per 100 g), making it a relatively safe protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions and without high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat and moderate saturated fat from deep-frying, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from the fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While whitebait is naturally rich in omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and calcium, the deep-frying process significantly increases total fat and caloric density; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions, preferring grilled or baked fish preparations as a primary protein source.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Whitebait (炸银鱼仔) is a popular deep-fried seafood dish served in Singaporean and Malaysian Zi Char restaurants and seafood establishments. The dish consists of whole juvenile fish — typically sprats or small herrings collectively called whitebait — that are lightly coated in seasoned flour or batter and deep-fried in hot oil until golden and intensely crispy. The result is a snack or side dish where the entire fish, including bones, is edible and provides a satisfying crunch. The flavour is savoury, lightly salty, and mildly fishy, with a rich fried aroma. Crispy whitebait is commonly served as a shared starter or accompaniment to rice at Zi Char stalls and seafood restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, it is calorie-dense due to deep-frying (approximately 525 kcal per 100 g), high in protein (~19.5 g per 100 g), and notably rich in calcium (~860 mg per 100 g) because the small bones are consumed whole. It also provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, vitamin B12, vitamin D, and selenium. The sodium content is moderate to high (~520 mg per 100 g), warranting caution for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is low (estimated 45), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without sugary condiments. The dish is halal-friendly and dairy-free but contains fish and gluten allergens from the flour coating.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy whitebait?
Is fried whitebait high in sodium?
What fish is used in whitebait?
How much calcium is in fried whitebait?

confidence_reason: No dedicated crispy whitebait entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried whitebait (FDC ID 175168), which is a close match for the preparation method. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature on deep-fried fish products. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of an exact Singapore/Malaysia regional source entry.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175168 (Fish, whitebait, fried). Sodium adjusted upward by ~10% to reflect typical restaurant seasoning levels. 2. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous deep-fried fish products from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for crispy whitebait was found in approved sources. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; deep-frying destroys residual vitamin C in fish. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy FDC 175168 and are not sentinel -1 values. 5. The food is classified under 'Fish and seafood' rather than 'Mixed' as the input suggested, because the dish is predominantly a fish product. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the fish-only protein source and standard cooking oils; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. The input description 'Per portionc' appears to be a typographical error ('Per portion') and has been disregarded in favour of per-100g standardisation.
